audits.excerpt: "If she did that, the pressure in the walls remained a problem instead of a prophecy."
audits.reason: The sentence converts corridor threat into conceptual framing instead of keeping Jaina in contact with stone, lungs, and the immediate bodily work of staying functional. It slips from grounded threshold pressure into authored interpretation.
audits.suggestedFix: "If she did that, the pressure stayed in the stone and out of her lungs for one more turn."

audits.excerpt: "No one in the corridor needed anything explained."
audits.reason: This summarizes the corridor reaction rather than grounding it in the functionary, Uther, and the hallway bodies already onstage. The beat briefly floats above the service passage instead of staying inside it.
audits.suggestedFix: "The functionary had already flattened himself to the wall. Uther was already looking past them toward the center of the palace."

audits.excerpt: "Any living man would have died there."
audits.reason: The line steps out of the room into generic judgment just after a strong physical hit. The prose already has the shaft, the wound, and the impossible closure available; it should stay on those instead of issuing a broad rule.
audits.suggestedFix: "The shaft stood deep in his throat. Blood welled dark around it, and still he did not fall."

audits.excerpt: "Seeing it happen made Jaina's stomach seize even though it had always been part of the plan."
audits.reason: This names Jaina's reaction and the plan in summary form instead of keeping the cost visible in Sylvanas's diagonal retreat and the bought lane itself. In a timing-critical fight, that level of abstraction thins the bodily price.
audits.suggestedFix: "Jaina's stomach seized as Sylvanas gave him the diagonal, one column base at a time, buying the lane with her own body."

audits.excerpt: "The room had become all distance and angles:"
audits.reason: The sentence reduces the aftermath to schematic language right before the prose returns to a much stronger concrete inventory of bodies and objects. It is redundant summary that briefly de-materializes the room.
audits.suggestedFix: Delete the summary phrase and let the concrete inventory that follows carry the beat.

audits.excerpt: "If he declared for them outright, half the palace would hear betrayal. If he left the captain to panic, fear would turn to chains and blades."
audits.reason: The thought drifts from immediate chamber evidence into generalized palace politics at the exact moment the spearpoints, captain, and dead king should be doing the work. The stakes are real, but they need a room-bound read.
audits.suggestedFix: "The captain's men already had spearpoints up. One wrong word from Uther and the room would hear treason in it."
